---
title: "Game of Thrones: Viewership Analysis"
author: "Rajat Dogra"
date: today
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 2
    toc-title: "Contents"
    theme: cosmo
execute:
  echo: false
---

## About the Show

*Game of Thrones* is an American fantasy drama television series created by David Benioff and D. B. Weiss for HBO. Based on George R. R. Martin's novel series *A Song of Ice and Fire*, the show premiered on April 17, 2011, and concluded on May 19, 2019, spanning eight seasons and 73 episodes.

Set in the fictional continents of Westeros and Essos, *Game of Thrones* follows several noble houses competing for the Iron Throne of the Seven Kingdoms, while an ancient threat stirs beyond a massive wall in the north. The series became one of the most-watched programmes in HBO history and won a record 59 Primetime Emmy Awards during its run.

![Game of Thrones title card (source: Wikipedia)](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/2/26/Game_of_thrones_title_card.jpg){fig-alt="Game of Thrones title card" width=500}

```{r}
#| label: setup
#| include: false
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)

got <- tibble(
  season    = 1:8,
  year      = c(2011, 2012, 2013, 2014, 2015, 2016, 2017, 2019),
  avg_viewers = c(2.52, 3.80, 4.97, 6.84, 6.88, 7.69, 10.26, 11.99)
)
```

## Viewership Statistics

The table below summarises the average number of US viewers (in millions) per episode for each season, as reported on Wikipedia.

```{r}
#| label: tbl-summary
#| tbl-cap: "Average US viewership per episode by season (millions)"
got |>
  rename(Season = season, Year = year, `Avg. Viewers (M)` = avg_viewers) |>
  kable()
```

```{r}
#| include: false
min_v   <- min(got$avg_viewers)
max_v   <- max(got$avg_viewers)
growth  <- round(max_v - min_v, 2)
pct     <- round((max_v - min_v) / min_v * 100, 0)
s4_s5   <- round(got$avg_viewers[5] - got$avg_viewers[4], 2)
s6_s7   <- round(got$avg_viewers[7] - got$avg_viewers[6], 2)
```

*Game of Thrones* debuted to an average of `r min_v` million viewers in Season 1 and peaked at `r max_v` million in Season 8 — a total increase of `r growth` million viewers, or `r pct`% over the show's entire run.

## Viewership Over Time

```{r}
#| label: fig-viewership
#| fig-cap: "Average US viewers per episode by season (millions)"
ggplot(got, aes(x = season, y = avg_viewers)) +
  geom_line(colour = "#8B0000", linewidth = 1.2) +
  geom_point(colour = "#8B0000", size = 3) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 1:8, labels = paste0("S", 1:8)) +
  labs(
    title = "Game of Thrones US Viewership by Season",
    x     = "Season",
    y     = "Average Viewers (millions)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

## Season-to-Season Changes

```{r}
#| label: fig-changes
#| fig-cap: "Change in average viewers relative to the previous season (millions)"
got |>
  mutate(change = avg_viewers - lag(avg_viewers)) |>
  filter(!is.na(change)) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = season, y = change, fill = change > 0)) +
  geom_col(show.legend = FALSE, width = 0.6) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#2E8B57", "FALSE" = "#8B0000")) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = 2:8, labels = paste0("S", 2:8)) +
  labs(
    title = "Season-to-Season Viewership Change",
    x     = "Season",
    y     = "Change in Avg. Viewers (millions)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)
```

## Observations

Viewership for *Game of Thrones* grew almost every season. The largest single jump came between Seasons 6 and 7, when average viewers rose by `r s6_s7` million. The one near-flat transition was between Seasons 4 and 5, where the change was just `r s4_s5` million. By the final season the show had attracted `r max_v` million average viewers per episode — `r pct`% more than when it first aired.
